candidate.title: Define proof envelope MIME type and serialization format
candidate.description: Register an application/bolyra-proof+cbor media type and define a canonical CBOR serialization for proof envelopes (human proof, agent proof, delegation chain). The current SDK passes raw JSON arrays of BigInt strings with no schema versioning or content negotiation. A COSE-style envelope with a version field, proof-system discriminator (groth16 vs plonk), and public-signals layout tag would let any HTTP/MCP transport negotiate format without out-of-band schema knowledge. Deliverable: ABNF grammar in spec/, CBOR CDDL schema, and a round-trip test in the conformance suite.

candidate.title: Complete DID resolution algorithm with on-chain state queries
candidate.description: The existing spec/did-method-bolyra.md defines the DID syntax but lacks a concrete resolution algorithm. Implement the Read, Create, Update, Deactivate operations per W3C DID Core §7, mapping them to IdentityRegistry contract calls: resolve did:bolyra:<commitment> by checking humanTree/agentTree membership and revocation status, return a DID Document with a verificationMethod containing the Poseidon commitment and a service endpoint for the handshake protocol. Include deactivation semantics tied to the existing IdentityRevoked/AgentTreeRevocation events.

candidate.title: Add humanTree root history buffer matching agentTree's 30-root buffer
candidate.description: IdentityRegistry maintains a 30-root history buffer for agentTree (preventing proof staleness during concurrent enrollments) but humanTree has no such buffer — any new human enrollment immediately invalidates in-flight proofs. Add an identical ROOT_HISTORY_SIZE=30 circular buffer for humanTree roots, update verifyHandshake to check humanMerkleRoot against the buffer instead of requiring an exact current-root match. This is a protocol-level parity bug: the IETF draft implies both trees have liveness tolerance but only one does.

candidate.title: Machine-readable conformance test vectors (JSON, 50+ cases)
candidate.description: Create spec/test-vectors/ with JSON files covering: valid handshake (human+agent), expired agent credential, revoked human identity, stale Merkle root (both trees), scope subset violation, cumulative bit encoding violation, delegation chain at depth 1/2/3 and rejection at depth 4, nonce replay, and cross-circuit nullifier domain separation. Each vector includes: named inputs, expected witness outputs (or expected failure reason), and the circuit/contract that should accept or reject. This is the minimum interop artifact any alternative implementation needs.

candidate.title: Add blinding salt to scope commitment to prevent bitmask brute-force
candidate.description: The current scopeCommitment = Poseidon2(bitmask, credCommitment) is deterministic over a 64-bit bitmask space — an observer can brute-force all 2^8 valid cumulative-bit encodings (realistically ~20 valid combinations) to recover the exact permission set from any on-chain scopeCommitment. Add a random blindingSalt private input and compute Poseidon3(bitmask, credCommitment, blindingSalt) in both AgentPolicy and Delegation circuits. Update the IETF draft security considerations to document the entropy requirement (128-bit salt minimum).

candidate.title: Cross-chain root sync via EIP-1186 storage proofs
candidate.description: Deploy IdentityRegistry on Arbitrum and Polygon alongside the existing Base Sepolia deployment, then implement a StorageProofRootRelay contract that accepts EIP-1186 storage proofs of the humanTree and agentTree roots from the canonical chain. This eliminates reliance on trusted relayers: any user can submit a storage proof from Base to update the mirrored root on Arbitrum/Polygon. The relay contract verifies the RLP-encoded account proof against the L1 state root (available via each L2's precompile: ArbSys on Arbitrum, L1Block on OP-stack/Base) and extracts the specific storage slot holding the Merkle root.

candidate.title: Off-chain session token after on-chain handshake
candidate.description: After a successful on-chain handshake verification, developers must manually manage session state. Add a `createSessionToken(handshakeResult, ttlSeconds)` that mints a compact JWT-like token (SD-JWT already in Python SDK deps) containing the verified nullifiers, scope commitment, and expiry. Subsequent API calls can pass this token for off-chain verification without re-proving. This bridges the gap between per-interaction ZKP auth and the session-based patterns every web developer expects, dramatically reducing gas costs for multi-step agent workflows.

candidate.title: Off-chain session token (JWT) after on-chain handshake verification
candidate.description: Every framework integration currently needs to pass raw ZK proofs per-call, which is 1.5KB+ of JSON and ~200ms verification overhead per tool invocation. After a successful `verifyHandshake()` on-chain, the SDK should mint a short-lived JWT (5 min TTL) containing the nullifierHash, scopeCommitment, and sessionNonce, signed by the verifier's key. Subsequent calls within the session present only the JWT. This drops per-call overhead to a signature check and makes integration with LangChain's `CallbackManager`, CrewAI's context, and AutoGen's `ConversableAgent` message passing trivial. Add `sdk/src/sessionToken.ts` and `sdk-python/bolyra/session.py`.
